attributes.stem:
A 77-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner to discuss the management of ongoing pain in her right knee. This pain is deep, aching, poorly localised and has been going on for many years. The pain is now present during rest and at night. She has been counselled on exercise, physiotherapy, walking aids and weight loss. None of these strategies have made much difference to her symptoms. She has tried diclofenac, paracetamol and even opioids, but they have not helped sufficiently. Her pain is increasingly impairing her quality of life and she is willing to do anything at this point to address this issue. Her history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes. She has a 30 pack-year smoking history, drinks two glasses of wine at weekends and has a BMI of 31.

She shows visible difficulties in her gait and significantly reduced knee flexion range of movement. There is crepitus on the knee, along with tenderness and bony swelling. Her vitals are unremarkable, with a BP of 140/100 mmHg, HR of 80/min and temperature of 37.2°C. WCC 9.89/L (3.6 – 11.0). An X-ray has confirmed changes consistent with osteoarthritis, with loss of joint space being the most apparent.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
The patient's symptoms are almost certainly those of osteoarthritis. This is shown by both the risk factors and the clinical manifestations. The patient's age, gender, smoking history, BMI, and comorbidities are all risk factors for osteoarthritis. This is an asymmetrical picture, where the right knee is impacted, there is a deep ache that is poorly localised, progressive loss of movement and loss of range of movement, crepitus, tenderness, bony swelling, pain at rest/night, normal vitals, and a lack of systemic inflammation (as shown by the WCC) - these are all characteristic of osteoarthritis. Other radiological changes consistent with osteoarthritis could be joint space narrowing, the presence of osteophytes, sub-cortical cysts and sclerosis.

First line management for osteoarthritis is lifestyle modifications. These include weight loss, exercise, a walking aid, and physiotherapy; all of which the patient has attempted and failed. Next-line management would involve pharmaceuticals such as NSAIDS and opioids - these have also been unsuccessful. Surgical management is indicated for patients with osteoarthritis once pharmaceutical and non-pharmaceutical management has been unsuccessful. The standard surgical management for osteoarthritis is arthroplasty. This refers to joint replacement, which is most likely to be recommended if the patient meets the following criteria:

  • Limited function of joint, with pain

  • Structural damage demonstrated

  • Failure of lifestyle and pharmacological approaches

  • Declines in quality of life

  • Notable suffering experienced subjectively

A final decision on surgery will obviously depend on a careful review of her state of fitness for the procedure.

Though she has not managed to lose weight, she has been unsuccessful here and hence recommended treatment would not rely on further attempts to lose weight. Though a walking frame may offer some value for mobility, it will not help with the pain and reduction in quality of life for the patient. In terms of surgical management, while osteotomy may be recommended for some patients, this is typically reserved for patients who are young, active and not overweight. In recent years, evidence has demonstrated that there is limited evidence for arthroscopy in surgical management of osteoarthritis. Arthrodesis of the knee is a rarely performed procedure, typically reserved as a salvage option.
learningPoints: Osteoarthritis is a common form of arthritis that can impact the DIP joints, and will typically affect the body's joints asymmetrically
Lifestyle management is first recommended, followed by pharmaceutical management and then surgical management
Arthroplasty is the most common surgical management for severe osteoarthritis.
references: Mandl LA. Osteoarthritis year in review 2018: clinical. Osteoarthritis Cartilage. 2019 Mar;27(3):359-364. doi: 10.1016/j.joca.2018.11.001. Epub 2018 Nov 16. PMID: 30453055.

Pradelli L, Sinigaglia T, Migliore A, Checchia GA, Franceschi F, Frediani B, Iannone F, Romanini E. Non-Surgical Treatment of Knee Osteoarthritis: Multidisciplinary Italian Consensus on Best Practice. Ther Clin Risk Manag. 2021 May 28;17:507-530. doi: 10.2147/TCRM.S288196. PMID: 34093017; PMCID: PMC8170371.

Raud B, Gay C, Guiguet-Auclair C, Bonnin A, Gerbaud L, Pereira B, Duclos M, Boirie Y, Coudeyre E. Level of obesity is directly associated with the clinical and functional consequences of knee osteoarthritis. Sci Rep. 2020 Feb 27;10(1):3601. doi: 10.1038/s41598-020-60587-1. PMID: 32107449; PMCID: PMC7046749.
